Title: Single-cell multi-omics in the medicinal plant Catharanthus roseus

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Use SLTr to improve flux from secologanin to strictosidine by coordinating transporter engineering with STR/TDC expression in epidermal cells; useful for pathway reconstruction and reducing loss of secologanin to secologanol. Caution: physiological function is supported by VIGS chemotype and coexpression/TAD colocalization, but direct transport assay was unsuccessful and downstream alkaloid levels were not substantially changed upon silencing.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes utilization/sequestration of secologanin for MIA biosynthesis; silencing causes secologanol accumulation consistent with blocked secologanin transport into the vacuole.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Hi-C/TAD colocalization with STR and TDC; epidermis-specific scRNA-seq expression; VIGS followed by LC-MS metabolite profiling showing secologanol build-up; co-elution with secologanol standard.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Young leaves for VIGS; mature leaves for Hi-C; leaf cell-type context; jasmonate discussed generally for pathway regulation but not as direct validation condition for SLTr.
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Not reported; gene lies in a TAD with STR and TDC, suggesting chromatin topology-associated regulation.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Leaf epidermis-specific expression in C. roseus.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Hi-C; bulk mRNA-seq; leaf scRNA-seq; scMet; BioProject PRJNA847226; Dryad 10.5061/dryad.d2547d851
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified as a MATE transporter adjacent to TDC in the STR–TDC cluster; proposed to transport secologanin from cytosol into vacuole where STR is localized; heterologous transport assay unsuccessful.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing altered secologanol accumulation but did not substantially change secologanin or downstream alkaloids, indicating possible buffering/redundancy and uncertain whole-plant productivity effects.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Likely tonoplast/vacuolar membrane-associated transporter; exact localization not directly shown in this paper.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SLTr was discovered from a STR–TDC genomic cluster and shared TAD. 2. It is specifically expressed in leaf epidermis where secologanin/strictosidine pathway steps occur. 3. VIGS caused accumulation of secologanol, supporting a role in secologanin transport required for efficient MIA entry-step metabolism.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Monoterpene indole alkaloids (including strictosidine-derived leaf MIAs such as anhydrovinblastine/vinblastine)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, especially epidermis-to-idioblast partitioned pathway; final bis-indole alkaloids in idioblasts
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense and major pharmaceutical anticancer compounds/precursors.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CrTPT2 is a candidate for engineering catharanthine export/partitioning. Combine with idioblast transport systems and late coupling steps to optimize bis-indole alkaloid production. Caution: this paper only cites prior evidence and shows catharanthine also accumulates intracellularly in idioblasts, so CrTPT2 alone is unlikely to explain final partitioning.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Previously proposed to export catharanthine from epidermal cells to the cuticle; current study indicates substantial catharanthine is additionally sequestered in idioblasts.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced prior study; current paper used scMet cell-type metabolite localization to infer additional transport beyond cuticular export.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not specified in this paper; previously linked to epidermal/cuticular catharanthine export.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: scMet in current study; prior transport study cited by authors
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as prior ABC transporter for catharanthine export to cuticle; current results imply additional intercellular transport from epidermis to idioblast.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating export may alter defense deployment or reduce substrate availability for dimerization if misdirected to cuticle.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane/cuticle export context from prior work; not tested here.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CrTPT2 was previously proposed to export catharanthine to the cuticle. 2. This study observed major catharanthine accumulation in idioblasts, indicating additional transport routes. 3. Catharanthine partitioning is more complex than simple cuticular export.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: STRTr is recognized as a transporter in the pathway and may be useful for pathway reconstitution with strictosidine-related steps, but this paper provides no direct characterization; prioritize only as a supporting candidate.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Identified as one of two transporters associated with the MIA pathway, but no new functional conclusion provided in this paper.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mention in pathway gene inventory/coexpression context only.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not detailed in the text provided.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included among 38 biosynthetic genes plus two transporters in the leaf coexpression analysis figure legend, but not functionally analyzed in the manuscript text provided.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STRTr is included as an MIA-pathway-associated transporter. 2. No direct new functional validation was presented in this study. 3. It should be considered a contextual pathway transporter rather than a newly resolved causal gene here.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots depending on branch

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Monoterpene indole alkaloid (MIA) / bis-indole alkaloid branch
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: THAS1 is a strong engineering candidate for improving anhydrovinblastine formation when combined with catharanthine/vindoline supply and the upstream oxidative coupling step. Because in planta VIGS gave no strong phenotype, stacking with THAS2 or editing multiple redundant ADHs is recommended. Monitor diversion to tetrahydroalstonine-related chemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the anhydrovinblastine iminium dimer to form anhydrovinblastine; previously known to reduce strictosidine aglycone to tetrahydroalstonine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: In vitro, forms anhydrovinblastine from the iminium intermediate and thus can catalyze the missing reductive step toward bis-indole alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Idioblast-specific scRNA-seq candidate selection; heterologous expression in E. coli; in vitro enzyme assays with peroxidase-generated iminium substrate; LC-MS product detection; VIGS attempted in planta.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product precursor anhydrovinblastine for vinblastine/vincristine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf idioblast context; in vitro assays used horseradish peroxidase plus H2O2 to generate substrate.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Idioblast-localized in leaf coexpression/cell-type analysis; part of tandem paralog array.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf scRNA-seq; scMet; genome assembly; BioProject PRJNA847226; Dryad 10.5061/dryad.d2547d851
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Despite strong in vitro activity, VIGS of THAS1 alone or with THAS2 did not substantially change AHVB/vinblastine/iminium levels, implying redundancy or buffering. Leaf tetrahydroalstonine levels are low, supporting possible alternative physiological role in leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential pathway competition with tetrahydroalstonine branch; redundant enzymes may blunt phenotype; engineering may alter other strictosidine-aglycone reductions.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Contributes to a late step in a likely rate-limiting coupling/reduction segment, but THAS1 itself not definitively established as sole bottleneck in planta.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THAS1 was identified from idioblast-resolved expression as a candidate AHVB-forming reductase. 2. Recombinant THAS1 reduced the iminium dimer to anhydrovinblastine with high specific activity. 3. VIGS did not produce a strong metabolic phenotype, so the physiological role is strongly supported biochemically but not genetically definitive.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor to anticancer alkaloids including vinblastine/vincristine.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Monoterpene indole alkaloid (MIA) / bis-indole alkaloid branch
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: THAS2 is a top candidate for boosting AHVB accumulation; combine with THAS1 and upstream oxidase engineering due to likely redundancy. Multiplex editing/overexpression in idioblast-targeted context is preferable to single-gene manipulation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the anhydrovinblastine iminium dimer to produce anhydrovinblastine; also previously characterized as a tetrahydroalstonine synthase acting on strictosidine aglycone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports formation of anhydrovinblastine in vitro and fills the missing reductase step in the proposed bis-indole pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Leaf scRNA-seq and coexpression-guided candidate discovery; heterologous expression in E. coli; in vitro enzyme assay with iminium substrate and LC-MS quantification; VIGS attempted.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product precursor anhydrovinblastine for vinblastine/vincristine.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Idioblast leaf cell context; assay substrate generated with peroxidase/H2O2.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Not reported; THAS2 participates in chromatin loop with PAS over ~50 kb.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Idioblast-specific in leaf; one of the idioblast-localized medium-chain ADHs.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Hi-C; leaf scRNA-seq; scMet; genome assembly; BioProject PRJNA847226; Dryad 10.5061/dryad.d2547d851
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Hi-C showed chromatin loop involving THAS2 and PAS; THAS2 also active in vitro toward AHVB iminium but VIGS lacked strong phenotype, suggesting redundancy.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential metabolic diversion and redundancy; uncertain in planta specificity due to prior THAS assignment.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Operates in a pathway segment inferred to be rate-limiting overall, but gene-specific control status not proven.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THAS2 was highlighted by idioblast-specific expression and candidate ADH mining. 2. Recombinant THAS2 reduced the iminium dimer to AHVB in vitro. 3. Lack of strong VIGS phenotype implies functional redundancy despite clear biochemical capability.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Monoterpene indole alkaloid pathway, alkaloid entry step
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: STR remains a foundational engineering target for strictosidine entry into MIAs; co-engineer with TDC and SLTr given genomic clustering/TAD colocalization and vacuolar transport dependency.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses secologanin and tryptamine to form strictosidine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides committed entry to strictosidine-derived MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Known pathway assignment; highlighted here by genome clustering, TAD colocalization, and epidermal scRNA-seq expression.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms central precursor for all downstream MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf epidermis context; jasmonate discussed as global inducer in background.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Located within same TAD as TDC and SLTr, implying chromatin topology-linked co-regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: genome assembly; Hi-C; leaf scRNA-seq; bulk mRNA-seq; PRJNA847226; Dryad 10.5061/dryad.d2547d851
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Vacuolar localization of STR is referenced to explain need for secologanin transport.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STR is clustered with TDC and SLTr in the linear genome and same TAD. 2. Its epidermal expression matches early alkaloid pathway partitioning. 3. The cluster aided discovery of the secologanin transporter SLTr.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots depending on branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and medicinal alkaloid scaffold production.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Monoterpene indole alkaloid pathway, alkaloid entry step
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TDC should be co-engineered with STR and secologanin supply/transport to support strictosidine formation in heterologous or native systems.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies tryptamine for strictosidine formation and MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Known pathway step; used here in genome cluster/TAD analysis and cell-type expression mapping.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies immediate precursor pool for strictosidine and all downstream MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Same TAD as STR and SLTr, indicating 3D coregulation association.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: genome assembly; Hi-C; leaf scRNA-seq; PRJNA847226; Dryad 10.5061/dryad.d2547d851
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Physically adjacent to SLTr in the cluster.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase / PLP-dependent decarboxylase
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Precursor-supply step; key control not specifically re-evaluated here.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TDC is physically clustered with STR and SLTr. 2. It shares epidermis-specific expression with early alkaloid pathway genes. 3. Its genomic context helped identify a transporter supporting strictosidine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots depending on branch

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CrPRX1 may support oxidative coupling chemistry but is a weak engineering priority for the idioblast AHVB step because its expression pattern mismatches product localization. Search or engineer idioblast-specific oxidases instead.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively activates catharanthine to form the iminium intermediate that can couple with vindoline.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reported peroxidase capable of generating the iminium intermediate in vitro/previously, but likely not the idioblast-specific physiological oxidase for AHVB biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression mismatch analysis via scRNA-seq and scMet; prior biochemical report cited.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms reactive intermediate preceding AHVB formation.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf epidermis expression contrasted with idioblast metabolite localization.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf scRNA-seq; scMet; prior biochemistry cited
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors hypothesize CrPRX1 is nonselective and has another in planta function; no idioblast-specific peroxidase was identified in the dataset.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpressing nonspecific peroxidase could create oxidative side reactions.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as physiological control step.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CrPRX1 had been proposed to activate catharanthine for coupling. 2. This study found CrPRX1 is epidermis-expressed, unlike idioblast localization of iminium/AHVB. 3. CrPRX1 is therefore unlikely to be the main idioblast-specific coupling oxidase in planta.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CS is essential for catharanthine supply; because catharanthine appears transported from epidermis to idioblast, engineering CS should be paired with transport modules to increase bis-indole output.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Defines epidermal production of catharanthine, a monomer for anhydrovinblastine/vinblastine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced known enzyme localization integrated with scMet localization of catharanthine.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms one monomer precursor of AHVB/vinblastine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Epidermal localization inferred from prior work cited in discussion.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Late monomer branch, upstream of coupling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Current study observed major catharanthine accumulation in idioblasts despite epidermal synthesis, indicating transport.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increasing catharanthine without coordinating transport/coupling may increase nonproductive sequestration or cuticular export.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CS marks epidermal synthesis of catharanthine. 2. Catharanthine nevertheless accumulates mainly in idioblasts, implying intercellular transport. 3. This transport step likely matters for bis-indole alkaloid engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf idioblasts predominantly, though synthesized in epidermis

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: D4H remains a key idioblast-localized vindoline-pathway engineering target; coordinate with DAT and monomer coupling genes to improve vindoline and downstream bis-indole alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates desacetoxyvindoline intermediate in vindoline biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports vindoline biosynthesis in idioblasts, matching vindoline accumulation site.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Known pathway role integrated with scRNA-seq/scMet localization; VIGS construct described in methods but no focal results presented here.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms late intermediate en route to vindoline, one monomer precursor of AHVB/vinblastine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Idioblast-localized (per prior knowledge and discussed in this paper).
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as evidence that vindoline is synthesized in idioblasts.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. D4H is part of the idioblast-localized vindoline pathway. 2. Its localization matches vindoline accumulation detected by scMet. 3. Vindoline synthesis therefore occurs in the same cell type where bis-indole products accumulate.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DAT is a key final vindoline-step gene and should be stacked with D4H and coupling genes for enhanced bis-indole alkaloid production; leaf-specific expression makes it useful for tissue-targeted engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls the final vindoline-producing step in leaves and aligns with leaf-specific idioblast metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Known pathway role; discussed in chromatin/TAD context and metabolite localization integration.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms vindoline, one of the two monomers for AHVB/vinblastine.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf-specific expression; contrasted with root acetyltransferases MAT/TAT.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Located in a separate TAD from root-expressed acetyltransferases, consistent with organ-specific chromatin-associated regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in leaves, not roots; late idioblast pathway context.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Hi-C; bulk mRNA-seq; leaf scRNA-seq; scMet
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of acetyltransferase tandem array separated into distinct TADs; TAD segregation mirrors organ-level expression.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant for vindoline formation, though not explicitly tested as rate-limiting here.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DAT is in an acetyltransferase array split across TADs. 2. DAT expression is leaf-specific, unlike root-expressed MAT/TAT. 3. This chromatin partitioning parallels organ-specific vindoline biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Monomer precursor for anticancer bis-indole alkaloids.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MAT is useful for root branch engineering and for redirecting flux between root and leaf alkaloid derivatives. Because it shows dual localization in root epidermis/cortex-endodermis, spatial expression control may be important.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acetylates minovincinine-19-hydroxy substrate in a root tabersonine-derivative branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to root-specific late derivatization toward hörhammercine-related metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Known pathway role integrated with root scRNA-seq and Hi-C/TAD analysis; prior in situ hybridization concordance.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for late modification in root branch leading to terminal root alkaloids such as hörhammercine.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Root tissue, especially epidermis and cortex/endodermis clusters.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Located in TAD 2 with TAT, distinct from leaf DAT TAD, consistent with organ-specific regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root dual localization in epidermis and cortex/endodermis (ground tissue-related clusters).
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Hi-C; root scRNA-seq; prior in situ hybridization
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Root scRNA-seq recapitulated prior dual localization by in situ hybridization.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Redirecting MAT activity may alter root-specific defense chemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MAT resides in an acetyltransferase array whose TAD organization matches organ-specific expression. 2. Root scRNA-seq confirmed MAT dual localization in epidermis and cortex/endodermis-related tissues. 3. MAT supports late root-specific MIA derivatization.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TAT can be used to engineer root-specific tabersonine derivative profiles, but organ-specific regulation and possible branch competition with vindoline-related flux should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acetylates tabersonine derivative substrates in the root branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Participates in root-specific late derivatization leading to hörhammercine-related alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Known gene in acetyltransferase array; expression interpreted from Hi-C/TAD and root scRNA-seq context.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Late branch tailoring step toward root alkaloid end products.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Resides with MAT in TAD 2, correlating with root expression.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in roots but not leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in acetyltransferase tandem array split across three TADs.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changing TAT may shift root defense chemistry and alter branch allocation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TAT belongs to a tandem acetyltransferase array partitioned by TADs. 2. It is root-expressed, unlike the leaf-specific DAT. 3. Its expression pattern supports chromatin-linked organ specialization of late MIA modifications.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: LAMT marks the leaf cell-type transition from IPAP to epidermis; useful as a spatial control point for reconstructing intercellular secologanin supply.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates loganic acid to produce loganin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Defines a transition point between leaf cell-type modules in the upstream iridoid pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Leaf scRNA-seq cell-type mapping of pathway transition.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms precursor toward secologanin and ultimately strictosidine-derived MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transition point from IPAP to epidermal expression in leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used mainly as a spatial marker in this paper, not newly characterized biochemically.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LAMT marks the switch from IPAP to epidermis in leaf MIA pathway partitioning. 2. This highlights sequential intercellular organization of the pathway. 3. The gene is useful as a spatial landmark for upstream-to-midstream MIA flux.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots through downstream products

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: NMT marks the leaf switch from epidermis to idioblast, useful for spatially targeted pathway engineering of late vindoline and bis-indole steps.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Defines the second leaf cell-type transition from epidermis to idioblast for late alkaloid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Late monomer branch step toward vindoline, enabling downstream bis-indole alkaloid formation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transition point from epidermis to idioblast expression in leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as a spatial marker for pathway handoff into idioblasts.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NMT marks the leaf epidermis-to-idioblast transition. 2. This confirms late vindoline/bis-indole pathway steps are idioblast-centered. 3. Spatial handoff is a major organizational feature of leaf MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor supply for anticancer bis-indole alkaloids.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TEX is a candidate control point for root-specific branching from tabersonine; can be targeted to redirect flux between root hörhammercine products and alternative tabersonine derivatives.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to late root epidermal branch derivatization toward hörhammercine-related metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Root scRNA-seq cell-type localization combined with previous pathway knowledge.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Late branch tailoring step toward root end products.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TEX2 has tandem duplicated paralog(s); cell-type expression helped distinguish likely functional paralog in MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux redirection may alter root-specific chemical defense.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TEX is expressed in root epidermis with other late hörhammercine-branch genes. 2. Root pathway organization differs from the three-cell-type partitioning seen in leaves. 3. Paralog-resolved scRNA-seq helps prioritize the likely functional TEX copy.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ISY paralog redundancy should be considered in editing strategies; multiplex targeting may be needed to alter iridoid supply robustly.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes formation of the iridoid scaffold in early secoiridoid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies upstream iridoid precursor pool; tandem paralog shows redundant ground-tissue expression helping explain weak silencing phenotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Paralog-resolved genome plus leaf/root scRNA-seq; previous silencing evidence cited.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool toward secologanin and all downstream MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf and root ground tissue/IPAP contexts.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Both paralogs expressed with pathway; in roots, both in ground tissue with one in higher percentage of cells.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: C. roseus leaf and root upstream pathway tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: genome assembly; leaf scRNA-seq; root scRNA-seq
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A tandemly duplicated paralog likely compensates, explaining why silencing of one copy had no obvious changes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Editing one paralog may have little effect; multiplex editing could affect overall MIA levels and plant defense.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The v3 genome resolved an ISY tandem paralog not easily interpreted before. 2. scRNA-seq showed overlapping expression patterns consistent with redundancy. 3. This explains weak phenotypes seen in prior silencing studies.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots via downstream products

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: GPPS LSU is a promising cell-type-specific precursor supply gene for engineering IPAP-localized geranyl diphosphate production feeding MIA biosynthesis; should be paired with corresponding partner subunit(s).
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Participates in synthesis of geranyl pyrophosphate for the MEP/iridoid pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides IPAP-specific precursor supply for MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Leaf scRNA-seq expression localization and comparison with GPPS SSU.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for monoterpene/secoiridoid branch leading to MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Contrasts with GPPS SSU expression in multiple cell types.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Boosting precursor supply without downstream balancing may cause off-target terpenoid flux shifts.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Potential precursor-supply control point, not explicitly tested.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPPS LSU shows specific IPAP expression. 2. This supports dedicated precursor production in the first leaf cell-type module of MIA biosynthesis. 3. Its expression is more restricted than GPPS SSU.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots through downstream products

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: GPPS SSU may support broader terpenoid metabolism beyond MIA-specific cells; engineering should respect its wider expression and possible pleiotropy.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Partner subunit in heterodimeric GPPS producing geranyl pyrophosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports precursor synthesis but is less cell-type restricted than GPPS LSU.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for monoterpene/secoiridoid branch leading to MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in multiple cell types, unlike IPAP-specific LSU.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Broad leaf expression in C. roseus.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Broader expression suggests additional roles beyond dedicated MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may perturb broader terpenoid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPPS SSU is expressed beyond the dedicated IPAP module. 2. This contrasts with the restricted GPPS LSU expression pattern. 3. MIA-specific precursor synthesis likely relies on cell-type-specific LSU partnered with a more broadly available SSU.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots through downstream products

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SS is useful as an idioblast marker gene for cell-type-targeted engineering or reporters, but may not be ideal for increasing anticancer alkaloids because it forms serpentine rather than bis-indole products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces idioblast-associated serpentine and serves as a robust idioblast module marker.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Leaf scRNA-seq coexpression network recovered SS in idioblast module; known serpentine autofluorescence marker.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms an alternative downstream MIA end product branch.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Part of an SS–SGD–SGD2 gene cluster.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A near-identical SS paralog exists in the cluster.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increasing SS may divert flux away from medically prioritized bis-indole alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SS was recovered in the idioblast coexpression module. 2. This validates the robustness of single-cell coexpression analyses. 3. SS is a practical marker of idioblast-localized MIA metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite with likely defense role; also marker of idioblasts.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MYC2 is useful for inducible, jasmonate-responsive activation of broad MIA biosynthesis, but it may not control cell-type partitioning. Best deployed with tissue/cell-type promoters or alongside spatial regulators to avoid nonspecific stress responses.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates MIA biosynthetic genes upon jasmonate elicitation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior pathway knowledge cited; current paper used coexpression analysis to show MYC2 is not embedded in the leaf cell-type biosynthetic modules.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent upstream activator of JA-induced MIA production, but not decisive for leaf cell-type-specific partitioning.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected but not part of the cell-type biosynthetic modules.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional activation of MIA biosynthesis under jasmonate signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Current study suggests JA-responsive regulation is distinct from regulators of cell-type-specific expression.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely pleiotropic stress/growth effects typical of JA signaling regulators.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Broad MIA biosynthetic genes (not individually resolved in this paper)
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely acts within JA signaling transcriptional complexes; not directly tested here.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates MIA biosynthetic genes under JA induction.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYC2 is a known jasmonate-responsive activator of MIA genes. 2. In this study it did not cluster with cell-type-specific biosynthetic modules. 3. JA induction and spatial regulation therefore appear separable.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots depending on branch

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ORCA3 remains a classic JA-inducible regulator for boosting broad MIA pathway expression, but should not be assumed to impose correct cell-type partitioning. Combine with spatially appropriate regulators/promoters for precision engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates MIA biosynthetic genes in response to jasmonate.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior knowledge cited; current coexpression analysis showed ORCA3 was not within the biosynthetic cell-type modules.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major JA-responsive activator of MIA pathway, but not the central determinant of cell-type localization in this dataset.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected outside main biosynthetic cell-type modules.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional activation of MIA biosynthetic genes.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Study emphasizes distinction between JA inducibility and cell-type-specific pathway regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential pleiotropic activation of defense pathways and metabolic burden.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Broad MIA biosynthetic genes (not individually resolved here)
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates MIA biosynthetic genes upon jasmonate induction.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ORCA3 is a known JA-induced activator of MIA biosynthesis. 2. It was not part of the leaf cell-type biosynthetic modules in this study. 3. Spatial pathway regulation likely involves regulators other than canonical JA-responsive ORCA3.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and roots depending on branch

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ORCA4 is a promising candidate spatial regulator for epidermal MIA metabolism and may be superior to ORCA3 for cell-type-focused engineering. It warrants direct validation before deployment.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with the epidermis coexpression module, suggesting a role in regulating epidermal MIA biosynthetic genes beyond general JA responsiveness.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Leaf single-cell coexpression network localization to the epidermis module.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Candidate condition- and cell-type-specific controller of epidermal MIA biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Leaf epidermis-associated in C. roseus by this study.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cell-type-specific transcriptional regulation of epidermal MIA biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf scRNA-seq coexpression network; BioProject PRJNA847226
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified as ORCA3 paralog with potential regulatory role beyond jasmonate response; direct targets were not validated in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown; broader ERF overexpression may perturb defense/growth networks.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Epidermal MIA biosynthetic module genes (specific direct targets not validated)
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Associated with positive regulation of epidermal MIA genes; direct effects unresolved.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ORCA4 emerged in the epidermis coexpression module, unlike MYC2/ORCA3. 2. This suggests a role in spatial regulation of MIA biosynthesis. 3. ORCA4 is a high-priority candidate regulator for future functional testing.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf epidermis-associated early alkaloid pathway, with downstream products in idioblasts
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Not resolved; possibly developmental/cell-type signals and possibly jasmonate-related cues
